input.command:
gh pr create --title "feat: skill / AGENTS.md miner from session corpus" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What

Direction #2 from the data-analysis ideation: turn the cross-session corpus into **reusable engineering assets**. A new **🛠️ Mine** button (next to 📊 Insights) calls `GET /api/mine?flavor=` and renders:

- **Proposed Skills** — codified recurring workflows (name · when-to-use · numbered body)
- **Proposed AGENTS.md Rules** — durable imperative guidelines with the evidence-based rationale
- **Tooling Gaps** — missing tools / friction the failures imply
- **Evidence** (always shown) — recurring tool-call workflows, failure→fix pairs, and the recommendation backlog the proposals were derived from

This is the **observe → act** step on top of #17's Insights: it converts the aggregated signal into copy-pasteable artifacts.

## How

- **`PatternMiner`** (pure, source-agnostic, fully unit-tested): deterministically mines consecutive tool-call 2/3-grams (candidate workflows), failure→fix pairs, and recommendations from sessions' normalized steps + cached AI summaries. Everything is counted by **session breadth** so one looping session can't masquerade as a fleet-wide pattern.
- **`SessionCollector`**: extracted from `InsightsService` (now slimmed to use it), killing the Antigravity-vs-`SessionSource` gather duplication the #17 review flagged. Shared `MAX_SESSIONS=150` cap; true total still reported.
- **`MinerService`**: gather → mine → one `MinerAdvisorService` (`@AiService`, shared `ChatModel`) pass to phrase the evidence. **Degrades gracefully** — returns evidence only with `aiGenerated=false` + an explanatory note when there's no evidence, AI isn't configured, or the model call throws (never errors).
- **`MineController`**: `GET /api/mine?flavor=`, `@ExecuteOn(IO)`.

## Tests

Every behavior change ships with a test, per project convention:
- `PatternMinerTest` (5) — n-gram recurrence, per-session counting, fix/rec rollup, empty
- `MinerServiceTest` (4) — AI-on proposals, AI-off, advisor-failure degradation, no-evidence
- `MineControllerTest` (2) — end-to-end over a temp `user.home`, exercises the no-key graceful path
- `mine.test.js` (7) — render (proposals + evidence), AI-off note, XSS escaping, empty, fetch ok/non-ok/error
- `e2e/mine.spec.js` (2) — button renders evidence; follows the selected source

Verification: `./gradlew build` (Spotless + JUnit) ✅ · 56 frontend ✅ · 23 e2e ✅

## Review

A code-review pass found **no MUST-FIX / SHOULD-FIX**. Both nitpicks applied: per-session counting for fixes/recommendations (consistency with the "×N sessions" label) and a `res.ok` check in `showMining`.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
Done. **#2 — the skill / AGENTS.md miner — is merged to `main`** ([PR #18](https://github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/pull/18), `9332e35`). Branch pruned, main synced.

## What you can do now
Click **🛠️ Mine** (next to 📊 Insights). For the selected source it aggregates the session corpus and shows:
- **Proposed Skills** — recurring tool-call workflows codified into name · when-to-use · numbered steps, ready to drop into a project.
- **Proposed AGENTS.md Rules** — durable imperative guidelines (with the evidence-based *why*) distilled from the failure→fix pairs — exactly the kind of lesson we hand-learned this session ("build needs JDK 25 via mise", "disable Ollama thinking").
- **Tooling Gaps** — missing tools / friction the failures imply.
- **Evidence** (always shown) — the recurring workflows, failure→fix pairs, and recommendation backlog the proposals were derived from, so every suggestion is traceable.

If AI isn't configured (or the model call fails), it degrades to **evidence-only** with a note, never an error.

## How it's built
- `PatternMiner` — pure, source-agnostic miner of tool-call n-grams + failure→fix pairs + recommendations, counted by **session breadth**.
- `SessionCollector` — extracted from `InsightsService`, which finally **kills the gather duplication the #17 review flagged** (both features now share it).
- `MinerService` — gather → mine → one LLM phrasing pass, with three graceful-degradation paths.
- `GET /api/mine?flavor=` + the `mine.js` panel.

**Workflow followed end to end:** branch → tests-first → `./gradlew build` / 56 frontend / 23 e2e all green → code review (clean; both nitpicks applied — per-session counting + `res.ok` check) → PR → CI green on all three jobs → squash-merge → branch pruned. **+20 tests** (now 13 mine-specific across unit/integration/e2e).

That completes #1 (observe) and #2 (act). The remaining direction is **#3 — the eval harness** (measure: turn sessions into a labeled eval set so you can A/B these mined rules and prompt/model changes). It's now well-motivated, since #2 produces concrete guidance to evaluate. Want me to start on #3, or pause here?
Yes lets stat on number 3

On to **#3 — the eval harness (measure)**.

My v1 scope, consistent with how #1/#2 shipped: a **deterministic analysis-quality eval**. It grades the analyses the app has already produced (cached summaries) against their transcripts using pure, fast graders — schema validity, actionability, conciseness, non-degeneracy, and error-coverage — and rolls them into a **scoreboard** (avg score, per-check pass-rates, worst offenders) labeled with the **active provider/model**. That gives you the measurement half of the loop: change model/prompt → *Recompute Analysis* (existing feature) → re-run Eval → compare scoreboards.

I'm deliberately keeping the **LLM-judge** (rubric scoring 1–5 for faithfulness/actionability) as the immediate fast-follow (#3b) so this PR stays a clean, deterministic, fast, fully-testable unit — the deterministic graders are exactly the cheap gate a judge layers on top of. I'll flag it at the end.

Let me branch and start tests-first with the pure scorer.

package io.github.glaforge.agybrainviz;

import io.micronaut.serde.annotation.Serdeable;
import java.util.List;

/**
 * The analysis-quality scoreboard for one source: how many of its cached analyses were graded, their
 * average score, how often each named check passed, and the lowest-scoring cases to look at first.
 *
 * @param modelLabel the AI provider/model currently configured, so two runs (e.g. after switching
 *     model or prompt and recomputing analyses) can be compared apples-to-apples
 * @param checkPassRates for each named check, how many of the evaluated sessions passed it
 */
@Serdeable
public record EvalReport(
    String flavor,
    int sessionCount,
    int sampledSessions,
    int evaluatedSessions,
    double avgScore,
    String modelLabel,
    List<NameCount> checkPassRates,
    List<EvalCaseResult> worstCases
) {}

input.prompt:
Review the staged git diff on branch `feat/analysis-eval-harness` in /Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer. Run `git diff --cached` to see it.

This adds an "analysis quality eval harness" to a Java 25 / Micronaut backend + vanilla-JS frontend that visualizes AI agent session transcripts. New pieces:
- `EvalScorer.java` (pure): deterministically grades one session's cached AI analysis (a JsonNode) against its transcript steps into an `EvalCaseResult` (0-100 score + which named checks passed/failed). Checks: schema-complete, has-recommendations, issues-have-fixes, concise-summary, not-degenerate (token-repeat / Base64 heuristic), error-coverage.
- `EvalService.java`: gathers a flavor's sessions via the shared `SessionCollector`, scores only those with a cached analysis, aggregates into an `EvalReport` (avgScore, per-check pass-rate as List<NameCount>, bottom-10 worstCases, and a modelLabel from AiConfig for A/B context). No LLM calls — fast/deterministic.
- `EvalController.java`: GET /api/eval?flavor=.
- Records `EvalCaseResult`, `EvalReport`.
- `FleetInsights.Session` gained an `id` field with a backward-compatible 2-arg constructor; `SessionCollector` now populates the id (from SessionSource info "id", or the Antigravity dir name). Verify this change didn't alter InsightsService/MinerService/FleetInsights aggregation behavior (they ignore id).
- Frontend `modules/eval.js` (`renderEval`/`showEval`) + a "🧪 Eval" button wired in app.js/index.html.
- Tests: EvalScorerTest, EvalServiceTest, EvalControllerTest, eval.test.js, e2e/eval.spec.js.

Focus: correctness bugs in the scorer (off-by-one in score %, the degeneracy/repeat heuristic, error detection), the FleetInsights.Session id change being behavior-preserving for existing callers, null/empty-safety on missing analysis fields, XSS/escaping in eval.js (it builds innerHTML — verify every dynamic value goes through escapeHtml; note numeric fields are ints server-side), and any should-fix issues. The project conventions: every behavior change ships a test, graceful degradation over erroring, pure source-agnostic aggregators.

Do NOT modify files. Return findings as MUST-FIX / SHOULD-FIX / NITPICK with file:line and a one-line fix each. If nothing material, say so.

content.text:
The eval harness (#3 v1) is built and fully green locally — I've launched a code-review subagent on the staged diff and will apply any findings, then open the PR and merge on green.

**What's built** — a **🧪 Eval** button → `GET /api/eval?flavor=` that scores the analyses the app has already produced against their transcripts with six deterministic, no-LLM graders (schema-complete, has-recommendations, issues-have-fixes, concise-summary, not-degenerate, error-coverage) and renders a scoreboard: avg score, per-check pass-rates, and the lowest-scoring analyses — all labeled with the **active provider/model** so you can A/B (switch model → *Recompute Analysis* → re-Eval → compare).

**Verification:** backend build (Spotless + JUnit, +6 eval tests) · 62 frontend (+7) · 25 E2E (+2). The one design touch worth noting — `FleetInsights.Session` gained an `id` (with a back-compat 2-arg constructor so no existing caller/test changed); the reviewer is checking that's behavior-preserving.

I'll report back once the review completes and it's merged.

<output-file>/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-scottdensmore-Developer-scottdensmore-agent-brain-visualizer/8619ea54-7ffc-44fb-96ff-4c199b70945e/tasks/a473ce30dded2d87e.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Code review the eval harness" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>The 2-arg convenience constructor preserves existing test/caller behavior (PatternMinerTest and FleetInsightsTest use 2-arg form). The FleetInsights.aggregate iterates `sessions` and reads only `steps()`/`cachedSummary()`, so `id` is behavior-preserving. Confirmed.

One last check: in `SessionCollector` line 72-75, `info.get("id")` could be null in theory, but that's the pre-existing id used for `transcriptJson(id)` too, so no new risk. And the Antigravity branch uses `dir.getFileName().toString()` which is non-null. Good.

Let me verify the `worstCases` limit=10 vs the doc "bottom-10" — WORST_CASES=10, `.limit(10)`. Correct. And ties in sort are stable. Fine.

Now let me confirm one real concern: **the `concise-summary` and `schema-complete` both depend on `summary`, but `not-degenerate` returns `false` (passes) for blank summary** — meaning an empty analysis passes not-degenerate vacuously (confirmed by test expecting 50 = 3 pass). That's intended.

Let me double-check the degeneracy heuristic naming concern isn't an actual off-by-one bug affecting the described behavior. The prompt flagged "the degeneracy/repeat heuristic" specifically. 6 identical tokens in a row trips it. That's a reasonable threshold; the constant name `MAX_CONSECUTIVE_REPEATS` is slightly misleading (it's "max consecutive occurrences" — trips at the 6th occurrence = 5 repeats) but not a bug. Noting as nitpick.

I have everything needed.

## Review: `feat/analysis-eval-harness` eval harness

I reviewed the full staged diff (scorer, service, controller, records, `FleetInsights.Session` change, `SessionCollector`, `eval.js`, and all tests) and cross-checked against the existing analysis schema (`AnalysisResponse`/`Issue`), transcript-step error fields (`FleetInsights.isError`), and all `Session` constructor callers.

**Nothing material / no MUST-FIX.** The core concerns you flagged all hold up:

- **Score %** — `(int) Math.round(100.0 * passed.size() / total)` is correct (3/6→50, 5/6→83); no off-by-one. `avgScore` `round1` is correct. Empty-set guard (`total==0`) present.
- **`FleetInsights.Session` id change is behavior-preserving.** The 2-arg convenience constructor delegates to `(null, steps, cachedSummary)`; the only 2-arg callers are `PatternMinerTest`/`FleetInsightsTest`, and `FleetInsights.aggregate` reads only `steps()`/`cachedSummary()`. `SessionCollector` populates id from the same `info.get("id")` already used for `transcriptJson(id)` (SessionSource branch) and the Antigravity dir name (non-null). InsightsService/MinerService untouched. Confirmed.
- **Null/empty-safety** — `EvalScorer` uses `path(...).asText("")` + `nonBlank` throughout; `EvalService` skips `null`/`isMissingNode`/`isNull` analyses and defaults id to `"unknown"`. Good graceful degradation.
- **XSS in `eval.js`** — every string field (`title`, `flavor` label, `modelLabel`, `sessionId`, check names/labels, `avgScore` via `String(...)`) goes through `escapeHtml`. The unescaped interpolations (`item.count`, `evaluated`, `pct`, `c.score`, `evaluated`) are all numeric — server-side `int`/`double` per the records — and `pct` is client-computed from them, so safe. The dedicated escaping test passes.

### SHOULD-FIX (1)
- **`EvalScorer.stepsHaveError` diverges from `FleetInsights.isError`** — `EvalScorer.java:118-127` matches only `status=="ERROR"` and `type=="ERROR_MESSAGE"`, but the canonical error detector (`FleetInsights.java:135-139`) also treats `type=="RUN_COMMAND"` with content containing "The command failed" as an error. Result: a transcript whose only failure is a failed shell command won't demand `error-coverage`, so a weak analysis passes that check vacuously. Fix: reuse/mirror `FleetInsights.isError` (add the `RUN_COMMAND` + "The command failed" clause) so the two error definitions stay in sync. Per the "every behavior change ships a test" convention, add a scorer case with a failed-command step.

### NITPICK (2)
- **`MAX_CONSECUTIVE_REPEATS = 6` is misnamed** (`EvalScorer.java:47`, used at :139). The loop trips on the 6th *occurrence* of a token (= 5 repeats after the first), not 6 repeats. Behavior is fine and tested; the name reads as "occurrences," not "repeats." One-line fix: rename to `MAX_CONSECUTIVE_OCCURRENCES` (or start `run` at 0).
- **`EvalReport.sampledSessions` is populated but unused by the frontend** (`EvalService.java:79` sets it to `collected.sessions().size()`; `eval.js` renders `sessionCount`/`evaluatedSessions` only). Harmless — kept for JSON completeness/A-B context — but if it's meant to be surfaced, `eval.js:renderEval` doesn't show it. Leave as-is or drop it.

No changes made to any files, as requested.</result>

</task-notification>

Review is clean — **no MUST-FIX**. I'll apply the SHOULD-FIX (unify error detection with the canonical `FleetInsights.isError` so a failed shell command also demands error-coverage) and the constant rename. Best fix: make `FleetInsights.isError` package-visible and reuse it — one source of truth rather than a divergent copy.

input.command:
gh pr create --title "feat: analysis-quality eval harness" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What

Direction #3 (v1) from the data-analysis ideation: the **measure** step. A new **🧪 Eval** button (next to 🛠️ Mine / 📊 Insights) calls `GET /api/eval?flavor=` and scores how good the AI analyses the app has already produced actually are — rendering a scoreboard:

- **Avg score** (0–100) and **evaluated / total** sessions
- **Per-check pass-rates** across the corpus
- **Lowest-scoring analyses** (with which checks they failed), so you know what to look at first
- Labelled with the **active provider/model**, so two runs compare apples-to-apples

This closes the loop on #1 (observe) → #2 (act) → **#3 (measure)**: change the model or a prompt → **Recompute Analysis** (existing feature) → re-run Eval → compare scoreboards.

## How

- **`EvalScorer`** (pure, source-agnostic, fully unit-tested): grades one session's cached analysis against its transcript into a 0–100 score across six deterministic checks — `schema-complete`, `has-recommendations`, `issues-have-fixes`, `concise-summary`, `not-degenerate` (token-repeat / Base64 heuristic — the exact failure mode the pipeline's prompts guard against), `error-coverage`. **No LLM calls** → fast, deterministic, cheap to run repeatedly.
- **`EvalService`**: gathers a flavor's sessions via the shared `SessionCollector`, scores only those with a cached analysis, aggregates into an `EvalReport` (avg, per-check pass-rate, bottom-10 worst cases, model label).
- **`EvalController`**: `GET /api/eval?flavor=`, `@ExecuteOn(IO)`.
- `FleetInsights.Session` gained an `id` (via a **back-compat 2-arg constructor**, so no existing aggregation caller/test changed); `SessionCollector` populates it so the eval can name its worst cases.

## Tests

Per project convention, every behavior change ships with a test:
- `EvalScorerTest` (7) — perfect/empty/degenerate/oversized/missing-fix/failed-command-coverage/check-completeness
- `EvalServiceTest` (2) — scores only cached analyses + aggregates; zero when nothing analyzed
- `EvalControllerTest` (2) — end-to-end over a temp `user.home`
- `eval.test.js` (6) — scoreboard render, empty-state, XSS escaping, fetch ok/non-ok/error
- `e2e/eval.spec.js` (2) — button scores the source; follows the selected source

Verification: `./gradlew build` (Spotless + JUnit) ✅ · 62 frontend ✅ · 25 e2e ✅

## Review

A code-review pass found **no MUST-FIX**. Applied the one SHOULD-FIX — unified error detection so `EvalScorer` reuses the canonical `FleetInsights.isError` (a failed `RUN_COMMAND` now also demands `error-coverage`), with a new test — plus a constant rename.

## Next (#3b)

This is the deterministic backbone; the **LLM-judge** dimension (rubric scores 1–5 for faithfulness/actionability, opt-in + capped, graceful-degrading like the miner) layers on top as the fast-follow.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
Done. **#3 — the analysis-quality eval harness — is merged to `main`** ([PR #19](https://github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/pull/19), `4459a24`). Branch pruned, main synced.

## What you can do now
Click **🧪 Eval**. For the selected source it grades every analysis the app has already produced against its transcript and shows:
- **Avg score** (0–100) and how many of the sessions were evaluated.
- **Per-check pass-rates** across six deterministic checks — schema completeness, has-recommendations, issues-have-fixes, concise-summary, not-degenerate (catches the token-repeat/Base64 failure mode), and error-coverage.
- **Lowest-scoring analyses** with the exact checks they failed — your triage list.
- The **active provider/model** it was scored under.

**The A/B loop is now closed:** switch `AI_PROVIDER`/model → *Recompute Analysis* → re-run Eval → compare scoreboards. No LLM calls in the eval itself, so it's fast and free to run as often as you like.

## The arc, complete
- **#1 Insights** (observe) → **#2 Miner** (act: skills + AGENTS.md rules) → **#3 Eval** (measure). Each shipped as one clean PR through the full workflow: tests-first → build/62 frontend/25 e2e green → code review (no MUST-FIX; SHOULD-FIX applied — unified error detection with `FleetInsights.isError`) → CI green → squash-merge.

Three follow-ups are teed up, in rough priority:
1. **#3b — LLM-judge dimension**: rubric scores 1–5 for faithfulness/actionability layered on the deterministic backbone (opt-in, capped, graceful-degrading like the miner). The natural next increment.
2. **Normalize Codex/Claude error text** (carried over from #17) so their "common errors" aren't over-granular.
3. **Persist eval runs** so scoreboards can be diffed over time instead of compared by eye.

Want me to take **#3b** next, or stop here?
Yes, take #3b next

On to **#3b — the LLM-judge dimension**. It layers rubric scores (faithfulness / actionability / clarity, 1–5) on top of the deterministic eval, **opt-in** (`?judge=true`), **capped + parallelized**, and **graceful-degrading** like the miner. Let me branch and build it.

input.prompt:
Review the staged git diff on branch `feat/eval-llm-judge` in /Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer. Run `git diff --cached` to see it.

This adds an opt-in LLM-as-judge layer (#3b) on top of an existing deterministic analysis-quality eval harness, in a Java 25 / Micronaut backend + vanilla-JS frontend. New/changed:
- `AnalysisJudgeService.java` (@AiService): `judge(digest, analysis) -> JudgeScore` rating an analysis 1-5 on faithfulness/actionability/clarity. Shares the ChatModel with AnalyzerService.
- Records `JudgeScore` (LLM output, @ReflectiveAccess @Serdeable @Description, int fields), `JudgedCase`, `JudgeSummary` (with a `notRun(note)` factory). `EvalReport` gained a `judge` field.
- `EvalService`: `forFlavor(flavor, judge)`. Deterministic pass unchanged. When judge requested + AiConfig.isConfigured(): judges a capped sample (JUDGE_MAX_SESSIONS=12) of analyzed sessions in parallel via the IO ExecutorService + a Semaphore(6), clamps scores to [1,5], averages, and returns a JudgeSummary. Degrades gracefully (JudgeSummary.notRun with a note) when: not requested / nothing analyzed / AI not configured / all judge calls throw. Builds a bounded "what happened" digest (USER/TOOL/ERROR lines, capped 6000 chars) from the transcript steps; reuses FleetInsights.isError.
- `EvalController`: GET /api/eval?flavor=&judge=.
- Frontend `eval.js`: judge section (rubric avg cards + per-case rows) or a "Run LLM judge" button that re-fetches with judge=true; `showEval(flavor, judge)`.
- Tests: EvalServiceTest (judge paths incl. clamping, cap, not-configured, model-failure), EvalControllerTest (judge opt-in + degrade), eval.test.js (button, rubric render, click refetch), e2e.

Focus on: concurrency correctness (executor submit + Semaphore + Future.get on the IO thread — deadlock/starvation risk? the eval runs ON a TaskExecutors.IO thread and submits MORE tasks to the same IO pool then blocks on them — is that a thread-pool starvation risk?), the clamp/average math, graceful degradation completeness, null-safety on JudgeScore fields, XSS in eval.js judge rendering (numeric fields are ints server-side; verify strings like comment go through escapeHtml), and any should-fix. Project conventions: every behavior change ships a test, graceful degradation over erroring.

The thread-pool starvation question is the most important thing to assess rigorously — compare to how AnalysisController does the same pattern. Do NOT modify files. Return MUST-FIX / SHOULD-FIX / NITPICK with file:line and one-line fixes. If nothing material, say so.

content.text:
I've launched the review. While it runs, the key risk it's checking — the eval runs on a `TaskExecutors.IO` thread and submits more tasks to that same pool then blocks on `Future.get` — is the identical pattern `AnalysisController` already uses successfully (Micronaut's IO executor is a cached/expandable pool, so no fixed-size deadlock). I'll confirm against the reviewer's findings, apply anything material, then open the PR and merge on green.

Local verification is fully green: backend build (Spotless + JUnit, +6 judge tests) · 65 frontend (+3) · 25 E2E.

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>All confirmed. The `steps` are immutable `JsonNode` lists read-only in the workers; digest is built per-task, no shared mutable state. Now I have a complete picture. Let me write up the review.

## Review: `feat/eval-llm-judge` staged diff

I focused hardest on the thread-pool starvation question, and compared the new code directly against the established `AnalysisController` pattern.

### Thread-pool starvation — NOT a bug (verdict)

The concern is real to raise but the code is safe, for the same reason the existing code is safe:

- `EvalController.eval` runs on a `TaskExecutors.IO` thread (`@ExecuteOn(TaskExecutors.IO)`), then `EvalService.runJudge` submits up to 12 tasks back to the same `@Named(TaskExecutors.IO)` `ExecutorService` and blocks on `Future.get()`.
- This is **byte-for-byte the same pattern** as `AnalysisController.summarizeConversation` (`AnalysisController.java:97` `@ExecuteOn(IO)`, then `executor.submit(...)` at :195 and `f.get()` at :247), which ships today with a `Semaphore(20)` and no bound on submitted chunks.
- Micronaut's default `TaskExecutors.IO` is an **unbounded cached thread pool** (`newCachedThreadPool`), and there is no virtual-thread/loom or executor override in `application.yml` or anywhere in the project. An unbounded cached pool always spins up new threads for the child tasks, so a parent IO thread blocking on child IO tasks cannot deadlock or starve — the pool grows to serve them. The `Semaphore(6)` caps *in-flight LLM calls*, not thread submission, so it can't self-deadlock either (every acquirer either releases in `finally` or the whole task returns).

If the deployment ever switched IO to a *fixed/bounded* pool this pattern would be a latent deadlock across BOTH call sites — but that's a pre-existing project-wide assumption, not something this PR introduces or should fix. No action required.

### MUST-FIX
None. The concurrency, clamp/average math, graceful degradation, and null-safety are all correct.

### SHOULD-FIX
- **`EvalService.java:239`** (`System.err.println` for judge failures) — the rest of the eval path is quiet, but `AnalysisController` uses the same `System.err.println` convention (:209, :290), so this is consistent, not a regression. Optional nit only: consider a real logger. Low priority.

### NITPICK
- **`eval.js:465-467`** — `s.faithfulness / s.actionability / s.clarity` are interpolated **raw** (not via `escapeHtml`), unlike every string field (`comment`, `title`, `sessionId`) which is correctly escaped. This is safe because `JudgeScore` fields are `int` server-side and clamped to [1,5] before serialization, and it mirrors `passRateRow`'s raw `item.count` (`eval.js:63`). If the judge object ever came from an untrusted cache these would render unescaped, but with the current server-owned int contract it's fine. No change needed.
- **`eval.js:463`** `const s = c.score || {}` — if `score` were absent, `s.faithfulness` renders as `undefined` in the label. Can't happen from the real API (`JudgedCase.score` is always populated), purely defensive. Fine as-is.
- **`JudgeScore` null-safety** — `clamp(JudgeScore)` reads `s.faithfulness()` etc. as primitive `int`; langchain4j structured output fills missing fields with `0`, which then clamps to `1`. No NPE risk. `comment` could be null but only flows into `JudgedCase` and is escaped client-side (`s.comment || ""`). Safe.

### Test coverage
Complete and matches project convention (every behavior change has a test): clamping (:684), cap at `JUDGE_MAX_SESSIONS` (:732), not-configured (:703), model-failure degrade (:716), not-requested, nothing-to-judge, controller opt-in + degrade, plus JS button/rubric/refetch and e2e. The `FORBIDDEN_JUDGE` lambda asserting the judge isn't called on the non-judge paths is a nice touch.

**Bottom line: nothing material to fix.** The starvation question resolves cleanly in the code's favor — it reuses an already-proven pattern on an unbounded IO pool. Ship it.</result>
<usage><subagent_tokens>53970</subagent_tokens><tool_uses>12</tool_uses><duration_ms>91883</duration_ms></usage>
</task-notification>

Review is clean — **no MUST-FIX**, and the thread-pool starvation question resolved in the code's favor (it reuses `AnalysisController`'s proven pattern on Micronaut's unbounded cached IO pool). The lone SHOULD-FIX (`System.err`) is explicitly consistent with the existing codebase, and the nitpicks are non-issues (raw int interpolation is safe — server-owned clamped ints, matching `passRateRow`). Shipping as-is.

input.command:
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
feat: LLM-judge rubric layer for the eval harness

Add an opt-in LLM-as-judge dimension on top of the deterministic analysis
eval. A "⚖️ Run LLM judge" button (in the 🧪 Eval view) re-fetches with
?judge=true, and the panel shows average faithfulness / actionability /
clarity (1-5) plus per-session ratings with a one-line justification.

- AnalysisJudgeService (@AiService): rates one analysis against a bounded
  "what happened" digest of its transcript (user asks, tool calls, errors).
- EvalService.forFlavor(flavor, judge): the deterministic pass is unchanged;
  when the judge is requested and AI is configured it rates a capped sample
  (JUDGE_MAX_SESSIONS=12) in parallel on the IO pool (Semaphore(6), the same
  pattern as AnalysisController), clamps scores to [1,5], and averages them.
  Degrades gracefully to a note when not requested, nothing is analyzed, AI
  is unconfigured, or every judge call fails.
- Records JudgeScore (LLM output), JudgedCase, JudgeSummary; EvalReport gains
  a judge field. GET /api/eval?flavor=&judge=.

Tests: EvalServiceTest (clamping, cap, not-configured, model-failure),
EvalControllerTest (opt-in + degrade), eval.test.js (button/rubric/refetch),
e2e. All green (backend build, 65 frontend, 25 e2e).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git push -u origin feat/eval-llm-judge 2>&1 | tail -3
input.description: Commit and push the judge branch
